readAloud.expectedAnswerFormat: Full text read aloud with natural pronunciation and fluency. No fixed expected answer; evaluated on content delivery, pronunciation, and fluency.

repeatSentence.expectedAnswerFormat: Exact repetition of the heard sentence. Evaluated on accuracy, pronunciation, and fluency.

describeImage.expectedAnswerFormat: Detailed description of the image covering key features, trends, and relationships. Evaluated on content, pronunciation, fluency, and structure.

retellLecture.expectedAnswerFormat: Summary of the lecture in own words covering main points. Evaluated on content, pronunciation, fluency, and structure.

answerShortQuestion.expectedAnswerFormat: Short, direct answer (1-3 words). See individual question answers.

summarizeWrittenText.expectedAnswerFormat: Single sentence summarizing the key points of the passage. See sample answers for each passage.

writeEssay.expectedAnswerFormat: Well-organized essay with clear position, supporting arguments, and examples. Evaluated on content, organization, grammar, vocabulary, and cohesion.

summarizeSpokenText.expectedAnswerFormat: Concise summary (50-70 words) covering the main points of the lecture. Evaluated on content, grammar, vocabulary, and conciseness.

tasks.prompt: Look at the text below. In 40 seconds, you must read this text aloud as naturally and as clearly as possible. You have 40 seconds to read aloud.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: Read the full text naturally and clearly.

items.title: Statistics
items.text: Statistics reflect vital information about the economy, the well-being of the population, and the environment. Society relies on statistics being visible, accessible and robust, and on statistically literate people making the best use of the information to determine future action. Statistical literacy, then, is the ability to accurately understand, interpret and evaluate the data that inform these issues.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: Housing
items.text: Housing fulfils the basic needs that people have for security, privacy and shelter. While the adequacy of housing is an important component of individual well-being, housing also has great impact on the nation's economy, with its influence on investment levels, interest rates, building activity and employment.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: Physical activity
items.text: Being physically active benefits people's health significantly, including reducing the risk of some chronic conditions, helping to control weight, and improving mental health. In recent decades, there has been a decline in physical activity because more people work in offices rather than in manual jobs.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: Leave of Absence
items.text: Students who wish to take a break from their studies will need to put in an application for Leave of Absence. If your application is successful, you will be notified via email. At the end of your Leave of Absence, you must re-enrol at Student Services and in the subjects you intend to study.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: Lie detection
items.text: There are a number of tests available which can suggest if a person is telling the truth, but knowing which ones are accurate is not easy. A newly created test is claimed to be the most accurate yet in lie detection. However, questions have been raised about its accuracy and ethics.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: Student exchange program
items.text: A student exchange program complements formal education, while promoting tolerance, maturity and independence – all highly sought after qualities in today's competitive job market. Living in the host country, not as a tourist or guest but as a member of the community, is what makes the experience both challenging and rewarding.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

tasks.prompt: Look at the [plan/graph/graph/table/graph/diagram] below. In 25 seconds, please speak into the microphone and describe in detail what the [plan/graph/graph/table/graph/diagram] is showing. You will have 40 seconds to give your response.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: Detailed description of the image: type of graph, axes/labels, key trends, comparisons, and conclusions.

items.title: Student apartment
items.description: Floor plan of a student apartment showing: entrance, living area with table/chairs/armchair, study area with desk near window, separate bedroom with bed/wardrobe/windows, small kitchen and bathroom.
items.expectedAnswer: This is a floor plan of a student apartment. The entrance leads to a living area with a table, chairs, and an armchair. Adjacent to the living area is a study area with a desk near a window. There is a separate bedroom with a bed, wardrobe, and windows. The apartment also includes a small kitchen and a bathroom.

items.title: Climate graph, Great Lakes Area, USA
items.description: Line and bar graph showing average temperature (°C) and rainfall (mm) across the year (January–December) for the Great Lakes region.
items.expectedAnswer: This combined line and bar graph shows the climate of the Great Lakes region. The line graph indicates average temperatures, with the coldest months being January and February around -5°C, and the warmest being July and August around 20°C. The bar graph shows rainfall, with the wettest months being June through September, peaking at about 100mm, and the driest months being January through March.

items.title: Energy consumption 2007
items.description: Bar graph comparing energy consumption by source (oil, natural gas, hydroelectric, coal, nuclear) for North America and South America in 2007.
items.expectedAnswer: This bar graph compares energy consumption in North America and South America in 2007 by source. In North America, oil and natural gas are the dominant sources, while coal and nuclear also contribute significantly. In South America, hydroelectric power is the main source, followed by oil and natural gas. The graph shows clear regional differences in energy preferences.

items.title: Language college timetable, week 1
items.description: Table showing a 7-day schedule with morning (9am-12pm) and afternoon (1pm-4pm) activities: tour, English classes, social activities, excursions, full-day activities, and homestay weekends.
items.expectedAnswer: This table shows the first week's schedule at a language college. The week is divided into morning (9am-12pm) and afternoon (1pm-4pm) sessions. Monday starts with a tour followed by English classes. Tuesday through Thursday includes English classes in the mornings and social activities or excursions in the afternoons. Friday has full-day activities. The weekend is reserved for homestay experiences.

items.title: Proportion of overseas-born people in Australia, 2000-2010
items.description: Line graph showing percentages of overseas-born people in Australia by country of birth (UK, New Zealand, China, India, Vietnam, Philippines, South Africa, Malaysia, Germany) from 2000-2010.
items.expectedAnswer: This line graph shows the percentage of overseas-born people in Australia by country of birth from 2000 to 2010. The UK consistently has the highest percentage, though it shows a slight decline. New Zealand maintains a relatively stable percentage. China and India show significant increases over the period, reflecting growing immigration from Asia. Other countries like Vietnam, Philippines, South Africa, Malaysia, and Germany remain at lower percentages.

items.title: Painted Lady Butterfly life cycle
items.description: Diagram showing the four stages: egg (size of pin head) → larva (black/purple caterpillar with yellow-green stripes) → pupa/chrysalis (reddish brown) → adult butterfly.
items.expectedAnswer: This diagram illustrates the life cycle of the Painted Lady Butterfly. It begins with an egg, which is the size of a pin head. This hatches into a larva, which is a black and purple caterpillar with yellow-green stripes. The larva then forms a pupa or chrysalis, which is reddish-brown in color. Finally, the adult butterfly emerges, completing the cycle of metamorphosis.

tasks.prompt: You will hear a lecture. After listening to the lecture, in 10 seconds, please speak into the microphone and retell what you have just heard from the lecture in your own words. You will have 40 seconds to give your response.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: Oral summary of the lecture in own words covering main ideas and key details.

items.title: Purpose of museums
items.audioScriptSummary: Museums need to move beyond being warehouses of old objects; they should have an educational purpose, engage visitors, and become instruments of social change by providing relevant exhibitions and services.
items.expectedAnswer: The lecture argues that museums should evolve from being mere storage spaces for old objects. They need to serve an educational purpose and actively engage visitors. Museums should become instruments of social change by offering exhibitions and services that are relevant to contemporary society and issues.

items.title: Graffiti as art
items.audioScriptSummary: Graffiti has existed for thousands of years (cave walls, Egypt, Rome); modern graffiti began in New York in the late 1960s with spraycans; subway trains became forums for expression; now embraced by art establishment.
items.expectedAnswer: The lecture traces the history of graffiti from ancient times, including cave paintings and Egyptian/Roman examples. Modern graffiti emerged in New York in the late 1960s using spray cans, with subway trains serving as mobile forums for artistic expression. Today, graffiti has gained acceptance and is now embraced by the established art world.

items.title: Biomimicry
items.audioScriptSummary: Nature provides useful concepts for technology; biomimicry = copying/imitating nature; examples: bright phone screens from butterfly wings, anti-reflective coatings from moth eyes, spider web strength, glow-worm light.
items.expectedAnswer: The lecture introduces biomimicry, which is the practice of copying or imitating nature's designs for technological applications. Examples include bright phone screens inspired by butterfly wings, anti-reflective coatings from moth eyes, the incredible strength of spider webs, and efficient lighting from glow-worms. Nature provides valuable concepts for solving human technological challenges.

tasks.prompt: Read the passage below and summarize it using one sentence. Type your response in the box at the bottom of the screen. You have 10 minutes to finish this task. Your response will be judged on the quality of your writing and on how well your response presents the key points in the passage.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: One sentence summarizing the key points of the passage.

items.title: Undersea noise pollution
items.text: Passage about how sea creatures use sound for survival, the increase in ocean noise from shipping over the past 50 years, and its harmful effects on marine life. Quote from Sylvia Earle about the 'death of a thousand cuts.'
items.expectedAnswer: The increasing noise pollution in our oceans from shipping over the past 50 years is severely harming marine life that depends on sound for survival, representing what Sylvia Earle describes as a 'death of a thousand cuts' to ocean ecosystems.

items.title: Chillies
items.text: Passage about the science behind chilli heat, how capsaicin works, evolution of spiciness to deter mammals and fight fungus, and research in Bolivia on hot vs. mild chillies.
items.expectedAnswer: Chilli plants evolved their spiciness through capsaicin as a defense mechanism against mammals and fungus, with research in Bolivia showing that environmental pressures determine whether chillies develop hot or mild characteristics.

tasks.prompt: You will have 20 minutes to plan, write and revise an essay about the topic below. Your response will be judged on how well you develop a position, organize your ideas, present supporting details, and control the elements of standard written English. You should write 200-300 words.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: Well-structured essay with clear introduction, body paragraphs, and conclusion.

items.topic: Students should be required to stay in school until the age of eighteen. To what extent do you agree or disagree with this statement? Support your point of view with reasons and/or examples from your own experience.
items.expectedAnswer: Sample essay structure: Introduction - present the debate and state position. Body paragraph 1 - arguments for compulsory education until 18 (foundation for careers, social development, reduced dropout rates). Body paragraph 2 - arguments against (some students benefit from vocational training, apprenticeship opportunities, different learning needs). Body paragraph 3 - personal experience/observations. Conclusion - balanced view advocating for flexibility while recognizing benefits of extended education.

items.topic: Environmental problems are too great to be managed by individuals so real change can only be achieved at government level. To what extent do you agree or disagree with this statement? Support your point of view with reasons and/or examples from your own experience.
items.expectedAnswer: Sample essay structure: Introduction - acknowledge scale of problems and state position. Body paragraph 1 - arguments for government action (policy-making, international agreements, infrastructure investment). Body paragraph 2 - arguments for individual responsibility (consumer choices, daily habits, collective action). Body paragraph 3 - personal experience/observations. Conclusion - propose collaborative approach where government and individuals work together.

items.title: Proofreading
items.topic: Difficulties of proofreading your own work; tips include knowing common errors, checking for logical flow and paragraphing, reading aloud, taking a break before proofreading.
items.expectedAnswer: Proofreading your own work is difficult because you often read what you intended to write. tasks.prompt: You will hear a recording. Type the missing words in each blank.
You will hear a recording. Click on the paragraph that best relates to the recording.

items.title: Telecommuting

summaries.text: Company leaders have to be careful... managers will find that a telecommuting arrangement will simply not work if workers feel isolated and excluded from the company culture.

Telecommuting has a lot of advantages but to make it work, company leaders need to plan in advance... training, security and communication... hire workers suited to working remotely... equal access to resources and advancement.
When workers ask if they can work from home, companies should consider a telecommuting arrangement... savings on overheads and training but only independent staff should be allowed.
Managers should not rush into letting their employees telecommute... if workers' needs are not catered for, company culture will be damaged and they may even be sued.

items.expectedAnswer: B

items.title: Consumer choices

summaries.text: While many of us believe that we enjoy making choices, several studies have shown that this is not in fact the case... consumers became overloaded and abandoned the choice.
The incredible range of choices... making business difficult for companies... consumers expect choice but give up without making choices if confused.
Consumers face more choices than in the past... shoppers are attracted if a number of options are presented... options still need to be good quality or they walk away.

With a wide range of choices, one would expect consumers to buy more... experiment found customers with many choices tried products but didn't buy much... with fewer options, more likely to buy something.

items.expectedAnswer: D
